binaries.title: tcpip.sys - FseProcessIncomingMessages kernel pool out-of-bounds write in FSE message reassembly (missing 0x400 bounds check)

changed_functions.name: FseProcessIncomingMessages
key_changes: Patched version adds explicit upper-bound guards `if (*r14 > 0x400 || rsi > 0x400)` before buffering an incomplete trailing fragment into the reassembly buffer at ctx+0x88 (the unpatched trailing-fragment path does `memcpy(rbx+0x88, rsi, rdi)` with no size limit).

Patched version adds accumulation guard `r8_6 = *(ctx+0x488) + rsi; if (r8_6 < result || r8_6 > 0x400)` (integer-overflow AND 0x400 cap) before the accumulate paths `memcpy(rbx+0x88+rbp_1, rsi, rdi); *(rbx+0x488) += rdi`, which were previously unbounded.

All new bounds checks are gated behind Feature_3924026683__private_IsEnabledDeviceUsageNoInline() (a staged feature/velocity flag), and on failure the code drops the data (result=0) and emits WPP trace codes 0x2f/0x30/0x32.
Buffer geometry confirmed: reassembly buffer at ctx+0x88, accumulated-length counter at ctx+0x488 => fixed capacity 0x488-0x88 = 0x400 (1024) bytes.

Message framing: 4-byte length prefix at buffer start, internal message length validated only against [0x21,0x400] (r14_1-0x21 <= 0x3df); the raw remaining-fragment length rdi/rsi was never validated against 0x400 in the unpatched code.
changed_functions.security_notes: Pre-patch, the FSE stream-reassembly accumulator copies an attacker-controlled number of bytes (rdi = *(arg2+0x38), incoming length) into a fixed 0x400-byte kernel pool buffer at ctx+0x88 without bounding the copy length or the running accumulated length (ctx+0x488) to 0x400. When the trailing bytes of a receive form an 'incomplete' fragment (rdi > 0x20 and the next 4-byte length prefix *rsi > rdi), the code executes `memcpy(rbx+0x88, rsi, rdi); *(rbx+0x488)=rdi` with rdi unbounded; likewise the accumulate branches do `memcpy(rbx+0x88+rbp_1, rsi, rdi); *(rbx+0x488)+=rdi` without checking rbp_1+rdi <= 0x400. This is a linear kernel pool out-of-bounds write with fully attacker-controlled length and contents, reachable from the network receive-completion callback OnReceiveComplete.
changed_functions.assembly_diff:
--- tcpip_unpatched.sys/FseProcessIncomingMessages.assembly (0x1401bd224) verbatim unpatched listing ---
mov  qword [rsp+0x18], rbx
push rbp
push rsi
push rdi
push r12
push r13
push r14
push r15
sub  rsp, 0x70
mov  rax, qword [rel 0x140228ac0]
xor  rax, rsp
mov  qword [rsp+0x60], rax
mov  rax, qword [rcx+0x20]
mov  rsi, qword [rcx+0x30]        ; rsi = data pointer (arg1+0x30)
mov  edi, dword [rdx+0x38]        ; edi = incoming length (arg2+0x38) ATTACKER-CONTROLLED
mov  rbx, qword [rax+0xa8]        ; rbx = FSE reassembly context
mov  rcx, qword [rel 0x140228268]
lea  r13, [rel 0x140228268]
mov  r12b, 0x5
cmp  rcx, r13
je   0x1401bd292
cmp  dword [rbx+0x488], 0x0       ; accumulated length counter
je   0x1401bd488
cmp  byte [rcx+0x29], r12b
jb   0x1401bd292
test edi, edi
je   0x1401bd4ef
... (WPP trace)
mov  al, 0x1
mov  rdx, rsi
cmp  edi, 0x20
jbe  0x1401bd4da
mov  ebp, dword [rbx+0x488]       ; ebp = rbp_1 accumulated bytes
cmp  ebp, 0x4
jb   0x1401bd36d
...
mov  r8d, edi                     ; 0x1401bd4da  r8 = rdi (UNBOUNDED length)
lea  rcx, [rbx+0x88]              ; dst = buffer base (0x400-byte buffer)
call 0x1401c2880                 ; memcpy(rbx+0x88, rsi, rdi)  <-- OOB WRITE
mov  dword [rbx+0x488], edi        ; *(ctx+0x488) = rdi  (no 0x400 cap)
mov  r8d, dword [rsi]             ; 0x1401bd494  r8 = *rsi next-msg len prefix
cmp  r8d, edi
ja   0x1401bd4da                 ; if prefix > remaining => treat as incomplete -> memcpy above
...
lea  rcx, [rbx+0x88]              ; 0x1401bd2f1 accumulate path
sub  edx, ebp                     ; edx = r14_1 - rbp_1
add  rcx, rbp                     ; dst = rbx+0x88+rbp_1
cmp  edi, edx
jae  0x1401bd336
mov  r8, rdi                      ; 0x1401bd301  r8 = rdi
mov  rdx, rsi
call 0x1401c2880                 ; memcpy(rbx+0x88+rbp_1, rsi, rdi)  <-- OOB WRITE
add  dword [rbx+0x488], edi        ; *(ctx+0x488) += rdi  (no rbp_1+rdi<=0x400 check)
...
lea  rcx, [rbx+0x88]              ; 0x1401bd37b else branch
mov  r8, rdi
add  rcx, rbp
mov  rdx, rsi
call 0x1401c2880                 ; memcpy(rbx+0x88+rbp_1, rsi, rdi)
add  dword [rbx+0x488], edi
...
mov  ecx, dword [rbx+0x488]        ; 0x1401bd463 label_1401bd463 process/reset
mov  eax, r14d
sub  eax, ecx
mov  r8d, r14d
sub  ecx, r14d
add  rsi, rax
add  edi, ecx
mov  rcx, rbx
call 0x1401bd108                 ; FseProcessIncomingMessage(ctx, buf, r14_1)
and  dword [rbx+0x488], 0x0
...
mov  rcx, qword [rsp+0x60]
xor  rcx, rsp
call 0x1401c2520                 ; __security_check_cookie
add  rsp, 0x70
pop  r15
pop  r14
pop  r13
pop  r12
pop  rdi
pop  rsi
pop  rbp
retn
(Full 41-basic-block, 754-byte listing obtained via diff_function_code side=unpatched; PATCH inserts at the equivalent sites: `mov eax,0x400 / cmp r8d,eax / ja <drop>` and `lea r8d,[rax+rsi] / cmp r8d,eax / jb <overflow-check>` guards gated by Feature_3924026683.)

changed_functions.name: IppCreateForwardPath
key_changes: Moved `*(Entry+0x78) = rax_1` (gsbase+0x1a4 processor value) to earlier in the function (before field init) - ordering only.

The single unconditional `*(Entry+0x70) |= 1` flag set was split into two Feature_277588282-gated sites: one set before RtlRestructureHashTable if the feature is enabled, and one set after if the feature is disabled - preserving the net effect but relocating it relative to the hash-table restructure/lock window.
Pure register renaming otherwise (rax_1->rax_3, rax_4->rax_6, etc.).
changed_functions.security_notes: This change only relocates a flag-set (Entry+0x70 |=1) and a field store under a feature flag; there is no change to allocation size, bounds, or lifetime. It is a behavioral/feature-flag reordering (likely to set the entry's 'usable' flag at the correct point relative to the hash-table lock), not a memory-safety fix. Not the patched vulnerability.

security_findings.entry_point: Network inbound data on the FSE transport channel -> registered receive-completion callback tcpip!OnReceiveComplete (indirect call at 0x1401bdfb9) -> FseProcessIncomingMessages
call_chain: OnReceiveComplete (0x1401bdf50, installed as a receive-completion callback pointer)
security_findings.vulnerability_class: Kernel pool out-of-bounds write (heap/pool buffer overflow, CWE-787 / CWE-120; accumulation path also CWE-190 integer-overflow-to-overflow)
security_findings.description: FseProcessIncomingMessages reassembles a length-prefixed message stream into a fixed 0x400 (1024) byte pool buffer located at offset 0x88 inside the FSE context object (ctx = *(*(arg1+0x20)+0xa8)), tracking the number of buffered bytes in the DWORD at ctx+0x488. The incoming chunk pointer is rsi = *(arg1+0x30) and its length is rdi = *(arg2+0x38), both attacker-controlled. The function validates the *internal* per-message length field (first 4 bytes) to the range [0x21,0x400] (r14_1-0x21 <= 0x3df), but it never bounds the raw fragment length that gets buffered. In the trailing-fragment branch (loop at 0x1401bd48a): when rdi > 0x20 and the next 4-byte length prefix *rsi is greater than rdi (message declared but not fully present), control falls to `memcpy(rbx+0x88, rsi, rdi); *(rbx+0x488)=rdi` at 0x1401bd4da/0x1401bd4e4 with rdi unbounded -> writes up to (rdi-0x400) attacker bytes past the 1024-byte buffer. The accumulate branches at 0x1401bd301 and 0x1401bd37b do `memcpy(rbx+0x88+rbp_1, rsi, rdi); *(rbx+0x488)+=rdi` without checking rbp_1+rdi <= 0x400, so a partial message left in the buffer (rbp_1) combined with a new fragment (rdi) also overflows, and the 32-bit `*(rbx+0x488)+=rdi` counter can wrap. The patch adds the missing guards (`*r14 > 0x400 || rsi > 0x400` and `acc+rsi < acc || acc+rsi > 0x400`) gated behind Feature_3924026683 and drops the connection on violation.
security_findings.trigger_conditions: Establish an FSE channel/connection that drives OnReceiveComplete, then deliver a crafted inbound byte stream. Two triggers: (1) TRAILING-FRAGMENT OVERFLOW: send a stream whose already-consumed messages leave a trailing remainder of length rdi in the range 0x401..(pool slack) where the trailing remainder's leading 4-byte length prefix is set to a value strictly greater than rdi (so *rsi > rdi and rdi > 0x20). This routes to memcpy(ctx+0x88, remainder, rdi) with rdi > 0x400. (2) ACCUMULATION OVERFLOW: first send a fragment that leaves rbp_1 = *(ctx+0x488) partially filled (e.g. near 0x400), then send another fragment of length rdi such that rbp_1 + rdi > 0x400 while each per-message header still passes the [0x21,0x400] check; memcpy(ctx+0x88+rbp_1, data, rdi) writes past the buffer, and *(ctx+0x488)+=rdi can also integer-wrap. Overflow length and content are fully attacker-controlled.
security_findings.primitive: Linear kernel pool out-of-bounds write with attacker-controlled length and contents adjacent to the FSE context allocation -> pool corruption leading to remote kernel code execution / privilege escalation (or at minimum remote BSOD/DoS). Because the write is contiguous and controllable, it can corrupt adjacent pool object headers/pointers for a write-what-where style escalation via pool grooming.

breakpoints.why: Function entry. Inspect rcx=arg1 and rdx=arg2. rsi:=[rcx+0x30] is the inbound data pointer; edi:=[rdx+0x38] is the attacker-controlled inbound length; rbx:=[[rcx+0x20]+0xa8] is the FSE context whose 0x400-byte reassembly buffer starts at rbx+0x88 and whose accumulated-length counter is the DWORD at rbx+0x488.

This is `call memcpy` for the trailing incomplete fragment. Watch r8d (=edi=rdi) as the copy length and rcx (=rbx+0x88) as the destination. If r8d > 0x400 the copy overflows the pool buffer. Also step to 0x1401bd4e9 to see *(rbx+0x488) set to the oversize length.

`call memcpy` for the accumulate path. Watch rcx (=rbx+0x88+rbp_1) as destination, r8 (=rdi) as length. Overflow if rbp_1 (value at rbx+0x488 on entry) + rdi > 0x400. 0x1401bd30c then does the unchecked `add dword [rbx+0x488], edi`.

Receive-completion callback that invokes the vulnerable function at 0x1401bdfb9. Break here to observe how the inbound network buffer/length are marshalled into arg1+0x30 / arg2+0x38 before reassembly.

watch: edi / rdi = inbound fragment length (arg2+0x38) - the value that must exceed 0x400 (trailing path) or combine with rbp_1 to exceed 0x400 (accumulate path)
rbx = FSE context base; rbx+0x88 = 1024-byte reassembly buffer; dword [rbx+0x488] = accumulated byte count (rbp_1)
dword [rsi] at 0x1401bd494 = next-message 4-byte length prefix (r8_12); set it > rdi to force the incomplete-fragment memcpy path
dword [r15]/[rbx+0x88] at 0x1401bd2e0 = per-message length field validated only to [0x21,0x400] (cmp eax,0x3df) - note this check does NOT cover the raw fragment copy
destination pointer rcx at the three memcpy sites (0x1401bd4e4, 0x1401bd307, 0x1401bd38b) vs. the rbx+0x88..rbx+0x488 buffer window
key_offsets: 0x1401bd2e7 `cmp eax, 0x3df` - the ONLY length bound present pre-patch (validates internal per-message length to [0x21,0x400], not the fragment copy size)
0x1401bd497 `cmp r8d, edi` / 0x1401bd49a `ja 0x1401bd4da` - decides 'incomplete fragment' and routes to the unbounded buffering memcpy
0x1401bd4da-0x1401bd4e9 - trailing-fragment `memcpy(rbx+0x88, rsi, rdi)` + `mov [rbx+0x488], edi` (no 0x400 cap) = primary OOB write
0x1401bd307 / 0x1401bd38b - accumulate-path `memcpy(rbx+0x88+rbp_1, rsi, rdi)` (no rbp_1+rdi<=0x400 check) with `add [rbx+0x488], edi` 32-bit counter wrap

In the PATCHED binary the added guards appear as `mov eax,0x400 / cmp r8d,eax / ja 0x1401bd705` and `lea r8d,[rax+rsi] / cmp r8d,eax / jb ...` gated by call 0x1401bd084 (Feature_3924026683) - these are the missing checks
debugging_aids.trigger_setup: Bring up the FSE channel that feeds OnReceiveComplete (an inband framed-message stream over the connected tcpip transport). From the peer, send a message stream so that after the framed messages are consumed a trailing remainder of length > 0x400 is left, with that remainder's leading 4-byte length prefix set greater than the remainder length (forces the incomplete-fragment buffering path). Alternatively, first send a fragment that leaves the buffer partially filled (dword [ctx+0x488] near 0x400), then send a second fragment whose length pushes ctx+0x488 past 0x400. Set the breakpoints above and inspect the copy length vs. 0x400.
debugging_aids.expected_observation: At 0x1401bd4e4 (or 0x1401bd307) the memcpy length register (r8d) exceeds 0x400 while the destination is inside the ctx+0x88 buffer; single-stepping over the call corrupts adjacent pool memory. Expect a pool corruption BSOD (e.g. BAD_POOL_HEADER 0x19, or KERNEL_MODE_HEAP_CORRUPTION 0x13A, or DRIVER_OVERRAN_STACK_BUFFER / 0x139 if the pool cookie is tripped) on subsequent allocation/free, with the fault trail pointing back into tcpip!FseProcessIncomingMessages. dword [ctx+0x488] holding a value > 0x400 after return is direct confirmation the bound was never enforced.
debugging_aids.notes: Buffer capacity is exactly 0x488-0x88 = 0x400 (1024) bytes; the accumulated-count field at ctx+0x488 is 32-bit, so `add [rbx+0x488], edi` can also integer-wrap. The safe/patched path is distinguished by calls to 0x1401bd084 (Feature_3924026683 gate) followed by `cmp ...,0x400` comparisons and WPP trace ids 0x2f/0x30/0x32 on the drop path; the unpatched path has none of these. The FSE context is reached as *(*(arg1+0x20)+0xa8); arg1+0x30 = data ptr, arg2+0x38 = length. Symbols: FseProcessIncomingMessages=0x1401bd224 (754 bytes), FseProcessIncomingMessage (single-message handler)=0x1401bd108, OnReceiveComplete=0x1401bdf50.

summary: The patch to tcpip.sys fixes a critical, remotely-reachable kernel pool out-of-bounds write in FseProcessIncomingMessages, the FSE framed-message stream reassembler (reached from the network receive-completion callback OnReceiveComplete). The function buffers inbound bytes into a fixed 0x400-byte buffer at ctx+0x88 (byte count at ctx+0x488) but pre-patch never bounds the raw fragment copy length: the trailing 'incomplete fragment' path does memcpy(ctx+0x88, data, rdi) with attacker-controlled rdi, and the accumulate paths do memcpy(ctx+0x88+rbp_1, data, rdi); *(ctx+0x488)+=rdi without checking rbp_1+rdi <= 0x400 (also a 32-bit counter wrap). The patch adds the missing `> 0x400` and integer-overflow guards (gated behind Feature_3924026683), dropping the data on violation. A PoC must drive the FSE channel and send a crafted length-prefixed stream whose trailing fragment length exceeds 0x400 (with a next-message length prefix larger than the remaining bytes), or split a message across fragments so the accumulated length crosses 0x400, to overflow the pool buffer with fully controlled contents.
